productIdea: A clinic scheduling workspace that helps staff manage provider availability, patient appointments, reminder rules, and conflict handling.
targetAudience: Clinic schedulers, providers, front-desk staff, and practice managers.
problemStatement: Small clinics often manage schedules through fragmented tools, which causes double-bookings, reminder mistakes, and privacy-sensitive communication issues.
constraints: Keep the first release local-first. Make provider availability, reminder wording, and privacy boundaries explicit. Avoid unsupported clinical claims.
desiredOutput: A build-ready markdown workspace with phased planning, provider availability rules, privacy boundaries, gates, verification files, handoff context, and enough clarity for a clinic scheduling tool.
mustHaveFeatures: Provider availability, appointment requests, conflict handling, reminder planning, schedule review, privacy-safe communication boundaries.
niceToHaveFeatures: Patient intake forms, cancellation workflow, waitlist management, provider preference settings.
dataAndIntegrations: Providers, appointment slots, appointment requests, reminder preferences, conflict states, clinic schedule notes.
risks: Reminder content may expose sensitive details, provider conflicts could be mishandled, and privacy boundaries could stay vague.
successMetrics: A reviewer can understand provider conflict rules, reminder limits, and what evidence proves scheduling safety.
nonGoals: No billing, no insurance integrations, no full EHR system.
timeline: Plan a realistic first release that proves the clinic scheduling workflow works before adding richer features.
teamContext: A clinic operations lead uses MVP Builder to prepare the scheduler plan and then hands the workspace to Codex, Claude Code, OpenCode, or a development team for phased implementation.

questionnaireAnswers.north-star: The first release must make provider availability, reminder boundaries, and conflict handling explicit and reviewable.
questionnaireAnswers.primary-workflow: A scheduler reviews provider availability, books or adjusts appointments, resolves conflicts, and prepares reminder content with privacy limits.
questionnaireAnswers.data-boundaries: Important boundaries include provider calendars, appointment requests, reminder fields, conflict states, and privacy-sensitive scheduling notes.
questionnaireAnswers.scope-cut: Keep provider availability, conflict handling, and reminder boundaries in v1. Defer billing, insurance, and deeper patient record features.
questionnaireAnswers.acceptance: A skeptical reviewer should be able to confirm reminder privacy rules, provider conflict handling, and explicit deferrals.
questionnaireAnswers.operating-risks: The biggest risks are sensitive reminder wording, provider double-booking, and hidden privacy assumptions.
questionnaireAnswers.deployment-guardrails: Keep the package local-first and force explicit privacy review before anything sounds production ready.
questionnaireAnswers.test-proof: Reviewers should capture conflict scenarios, reminder wording checks, and observed scheduling outcomes before advancement.
questionnaireAnswers.failure-modes: Double-booking when provider availability is not checked, reminder content leaking sensitive details, cancellation not updating waitlist.
questionnaireAnswers.observability: Track booking success rate, cancellation rate, conflict resolution time, and reminder delivery status.
questionnaireAnswers.scaling-risk: Concurrent bookings for the same provider slot, reminder volume spikes, and growing patient lists.
